title: How Data Flows Through Your Application
subtitle: Controller → Service → Repository → Database — the layered architecture

sections.title: The Layered Architecture
sections.text:
Professional backend applications use a layered architecture to separate concerns. The most common pattern is a three-layer model: Controller → Service → Repository. Each layer has a specific responsibility:

  • Controller — Handles HTTP request/response. Parses input, validates format, returns status codes. No business logic.
  • Service — Contains business rules, orchestrates operations, enforces policies.
  • Repository — Abstracts data access. Talks to databases, APIs, or files. Only one layer touches the database.

This separation makes your code testable, maintainable, and swappable. You can switch databases without touching business logic.
sections.caption: Request flows through Controller → Service → Repository → Database and back
sections.callout: Keep each layer focused on one job. A controller that queries the database directly is a maintainability nightmare. Layer separation is worth the extra files.

sections.title: The Food Delivery Example
sections.text:
Let's trace a complete flow through the layers using a real example: a food delivery app submitting an order.

  1. Controller receives POST /orders with the cart items, validates the JSON format, and passes the parsed order to the service.
  2. Service calculates the total, checks inventory, applies discounts, verifies the payment, and calls the repository.
  3. Repository inserts the order into the database, creates order-item records, and returns the created order ID.
  4. The response flows back through each layer: repository returns data, service formats it, controller sends the 201 response.
sections.caption: An order submission flows through all three layers before returning the response
sections.detail: This layered approach shines as your application grows. When you need to add audit logging, you add it in the service layer — not scattered across controllers. When you migrate from PostgreSQL to MongoDB, you only change the repository layer. The controller and service never know the database changed. This is the power of dependency inversion and separation of concerns.
